\begin{document}

\title{Phase Concentration of Hemispherical Monument Architecture\\
at 3\textdegree{} Longitude Intervals: An Exploratory Study\\
of UNESCO and Wikidata Stupa Corpora}

\author{Seth Tenenbaum\thanks{ORCID: \href{https://orcid.org/0009-0008-5797-2498}{0009-0008-5797-2498}.
Correspondence: seth@fourthtemple.com}\\
Independent Scholar, USA}

\maketitle

\begin{abstract}
This paper evaluates whether hemispherical monument architecture shows
longitude-phase concentration at a candidate 3\textdegree{} interval
relative to the $\sim$35\textdegree{}E corridor. Three corpora
assembled by three independent processes all recover the same signal.
The UNESCO Cultural/Mixed World Heritage dome/stupa subset
($N=\NcircTotal$), extracted from institutional XML metadata, shows
Rayleigh phase concentration ($R=\circConcDomeR{}$,
perm-$p=\circConcDomeP{}$, \circConcDomePSig{}) and is the most
geographically distributed of the three corpora, with A-tier sites
spanning the full Eurasian corridor. The Wikidata Q180987 stupa corpus
($N=\npcNStupa{}$), independently curated with no researcher
involvement, shows significant phase concentration
($R=\circConcStupaR{}$, perm-$p=\circConcStupaP{}$,
\circConcStupaPSig{}); leave-cluster-out analysis shows this result
is substantially carried by the Java/Borobudur node, reflecting the
documented historical concentration of stupa construction around that
complex. A filtered OpenStreetMap stupa layer
(\texttt{building/historic/ruins=stupa}, $N=\osmStupaTotal$),
assembled by crowd-sourced structural tagging independent of both
UNESCO and Wikidata, independently replicates the signal
($R=\osmStupaRayleighR{}$, perm-$p=\osmStupaRayleighP{}$).
All three recover the same $\sim$35\textdegree{}E anchor
($K \geq \npcOsmStupaK{}$). The full UNESCO Cultural/Mixed
corpus shows no global Rayleigh periodicity at this period
($R=\circConcUnescoR{}$, perm-$p=\circConcUnescoP{}$, ns), and
Watson's $U^2$/Kuiper tests confirm the dome/stupa phase distribution
differs from the full corpus ($p=\circWatsonPUnescoStupa{}$,
$p=\circKuiperPUnescoStupa{}$), establishing type-specificity. Null~A
($p=\geoNullDomeBootP$) locates the UNESCO dome enrichment in the
subset's own longitude geography; Null~C shows the broader same-footprint
UNESCO background does not replicate the dome A++ count. The UNESCO
results are conditional because the 3\textdegree{} frame was identified
there first; the Wikidata and OSM results carry no such conditioning.
Negative controls confirm the signal is not a generic property of
Eurasian geography: OWTRAD Silk Road nodes and the Americas sub-corpus
do not recover the monument anchor. The result is best read as a
modest, geographically conditioned, mechanism-free phase concentration
signal in hemispherical architecture, with the three corpora
illuminating different aspects: distributed alignment across the
corridor (UNESCO), node dominance at the major historical center
(Wikidata/Borobudur), and crowd-sourced corroboration (OSM). It is
not evidence for a universal grid, historical intentionality, ancient
metrology, or a unique Gerizim origin.
\end{abstract}

\bigskip

\noindent\textbf{Keywords:}
spatial statistics, circular statistics, stupa, domed monuments,
UNESCO World Heritage, 3-degree harmonics, selected-frame analysis,
monument distribution, phase concentration, Eurasian corridor

\bigskip
